ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a jellyfish food product class (FOODON_03311737) but no specific entry for jellyfish combined with iceplant (Mesembryanthemum crystallinum). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers jellyfish as a seafood commodity. No composite code exists for jellyfish-iceplant salad. Closest parent used for the dominant ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for jellyfish food product or jellyfish-iceplant salad as of April 2026.

preparation_method: dressed with sesame oil and vinegar

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical shared serving of jellyfish and iceplant salad at a Chinese restaurant is approximately 200 g, presented as a cold appetiser platter.

nutrient_source.title: Jellyfish, dried, salted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15244) and Mesembryanthemum crystallinum / iceplant composition (FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table); composite estimate for dressed salad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish jellyfish-iceplant salad. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for prepared jellyfish (NDB 15244: ~11 kcal, 2.6 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 0.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with iceplant composition (approximately 17 kcal, 1.0 g protein, 0.2 g fat, 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g per FAO/INFOODS SEA) and a standard sesame-vinegar dressing contribution. The final composite per-100 g values reflect an approximate 55% jellyfish / 35% iceplant / 10% dressing ratio typical of restaurant preparation. Sodium is elevated due to residual salt from jellyfish processing. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of jellyfish and low-carbohydrate seafood salads; analogous estimate based on GI of marinated seafood and non-starchy vegetable salads (University of Sydney GI Database)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for jellyfish-iceplant salad. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entries for marinated seafood dishes and non-starchy vegetable salads, which consistently fall in the 10–20 range. Iceplant is a non-starchy succulent with negligible glycaemic impact. Jellyfish contributes minimal carbohydrate. The sesame-vinegar dressing may slightly lower GI via acetic acid effect (Liljeberg & Björck, 1998, AJCN). GL per 200 g serving = (15 × 2.2 × 2.0) / 100 ≈ 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High residual sodium from salted jellyfish processing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request a low-sodium preparation, as a 200 g serving contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 37% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and only 2.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a negligible glycaemic load and is generally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though sodium content warrants attention in those with comorbid hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from jellyfish processing offset by low saturated fat and beneficial sesame oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat and contains heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from sesame oil, but the high sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g) is a concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease who should adhere to sodium-restricted diets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jellyfish and Iceplant Salad (冰菜拌海蜇, Bīng cài bàn hǎizhé) is a Chinese cold appetiser combining two distinctive ingredients: processed jellyfish and iceplant (Mesembryanthemum crystallinum). Jellyfish used in Chinese cuisine is typically salted during processing to preserve it, then desalted by repeated soaking before being blanched briefly in hot water and chilled. The result is a translucent, firm, and pleasantly crunchy ingredient with a mild oceanic flavour. Iceplant is a succulent plant whose leaves are covered in glistening water-filled cells that burst with a refreshing, slightly saline crunch when eaten. The two ingredients are tossed together with a classic Chinese cold-dish dressing of sesame oil, rice vinegar, light soy sauce, minced garlic, and sometimes chilli oil or sugar, producing a dish that is simultaneously cool, tangy, nutty, and lightly saline. The dish is served cold as a starter or shared appetiser in Chinese restaurants, including hot-pot establishments in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is very low in calories (approximately 28 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrates (2.2 g per 100 g), and provides modest protein (2.1 g per 100 g) from the jellyfish collagen. The primary clinical concern is sodium, which can reach 420 mg per 100 g due to residual salt from jellyfish processing. The glycemic index is estimated at 15 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is not suitable for strict halal diets without verified halal-certified jellyfish, and contains soy and sesame allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in jellyfish iceplant?
Is 冰菜拌海蜇 good for diabetics?
What does jellyfish taste like in Chinese salad?
Is jellyfish salad high in sodium?
What is iceplant in Chinese food?
Can I eat jellyfish salad if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of jellyfish salad?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for prepared jellyfish and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for iceplant, combined using a representative ingredient ratio. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because both component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae), vitamin B1 (vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg), vitamin B2 (vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg), vitamin B3 (vitamin_b3_niacin_mg), vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg), vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg), folate (folate_mcg_dfe), vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg), vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg), phosphorus (phosphorus_mg), magnesium (magnesium_mg), zinc (zinc_mg), and selenium (selenium_mcg) are all set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the component source entries used for this composite estimate (USDA SR Legacy NDB 15244 for jellyfish and FAO/INFOODS SEA for iceplant) and cannot be reliably derived without a direct composite analysis. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero: jellyfish and iceplant are naturally free of industrial trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for the jellyfish component). Sodium is elevated due to residual salt from commercial jellyfish processing even after desalting; restaurant preparations vary. Halal status is marked false as jellyfish halal certification is not universally established and the source restaurant has not confirmed halal status. GI set to 15 as an analogous estimate (low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for marinated seafood and non-starchy vegetable salads; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the approved literature.
